title: Publications
text:
Peer Review Activity
Reviewer for Molecular Ecology

title: Research
text: My work combines evolutionary biology, comparative morphology, and functional genomics to understand how complex phenotypes arise and are modified over evolutionary time. I primarily use threespine stickleback fish as a model system, complemented by earlier projects in vertebrate palaeontology and bird domestication.

title: Research
text:
PhD Research
Department of Environmental Sciences, Universität Basel · June 2021 – September 2025
Supervised by Dr. Daniel Berner
Objective: Determining the genomic and transcriptomic causes of target phenotypes.
My doctoral research aimed to identify the regulatory and coding mechanisms underlying morphological adaptation in stickleback fish. Key activities included:

Processed large-scale genomic datasets using high-performance computing (HPC) clusters
Designed and executed complementation crosses to test candidate loci
Identified developmental divergence stages via skeletal double-staining
Performed tissue sampling for bulk RNA-seq, single-cell RNA-seq, and single-cell ATAC-seq
Integrated multi-omics data to identify candidate regulatory elements and genes underlying morphological trait loss

Focal systems:


Dorsal Spine Evolution
Investigating the regulatory basis of dorsal spine number reduction in freshwater stickleback populations, with a focus on hoxdb gene regulation.


Pelvic Armor Reduction
Studying the developmental genetic mechanisms behind pelvic armor loss in stickleback populations adapted to acidic environments.

title: Research
text:
MSc Research
Paläontologisches Institut, Universität Zürich · September 2019 – June 2021
Supervised by Prof. Dr. Marcelo Sánchez-Villagra

Project 1 · Domestication and Development in Birds
Investigated how domestication affects morphological development in domestic birds, with a focus on skeletal variation in chickens compared to wild relatives (mallard and Muscovy ducks). This project combined 2D/3D X-ray imaging, whole-mount skeletal staining, landmark selection, and Procrustes analyses for morphometrics.


Project 2 · Fossil Description of Laquintasaura venezuelae
Described newly recovered fossil material from an early ornithischian dinosaur, Laquintasaura venezuelae (Early Jurassic, Venezuela), including the first non-invasive imaging of a previously undescribed cranial element.

title: Research
text:
Technical Skills


Molecular & Wet Lab

Sample fixation, DNA and RNA extraction
PCR, electrophoresis QC, library preparation
Ion chromatography, enzymatic kinetics
Northern, Southern, and Western blotting
Whole-mount skeletal staining



Computational & Imaging

R (genomic & transcriptomic pipelines)
STAR align (RNA-seq), NovoAlign, bwa-mem2 (genomic aligners)
AlphaFold (protein structure prediction)
Mimics (3D scan processing)
High-Performance Computing (HPC)
scRNA-seq + scATAC-seq analysis
2D/3D X-ray imaging, Procrustes morphometrics

Reviewer for Molecular Ecology

title: Dr. Carlos Manuel Herrera Castillo
text:
I am an evolutionary biologist specialising in the genomic and transcriptomic basis of morphological adaptation. My doctoral work at Universität Basel focused on identifying the regulatory mechanisms driving phenotypic divergence in threespine stickleback fish (Gasterosteus aculeatus), combining large-scale genomic datasets, multi-omics approaches, and experimental genetics.
More broadly, my research sits at the intersection of evolutionary biology, developmental genetics, and comparative morphology — from vertebrate palaeontology to single-cell genomics.

title: Dr. Carlos Manuel Herrera Castillo
text:

My doctoral work at Universität Basel focused on identifying the regulatory mechanisms driving phenotypic divergence in threespine stickleback fish (Gasterosteus aculeatus), combining large-scale genomic datasets, multi-omics approaches, and experimental genetics.
More broadly, my research sits at the intersection of evolutionary biology, developmental genetics, and comparative morphology — from vertebrate palaeontology to single-cell genomics.

title: Dr. Carlos Manuel Herrera Castillo
text:
Current Position
I am currently a Lecturer in Introduction to Scientific Work at Universität Zürich (06.2025 – Present), while continuing collaborative research on stickleback evolution and development.
